aggregate_governed_rationale: Binary classification; per-item baselines are necessarily 0%/100%; powerability governed by aggregate baseline + paired McNemar (which ignores concordant both-correct ceiling pairs, so ceiling items cannot inflate positive lift; floor items are the discriminative set); aggregate baseline 57.4% = in-band/poweable
source: Combined from 4 source files. authored: theory-of-constraints-bottleneck, margin-of-safety-provision; harder-systems items from evals/datasets/calibrated/systems-product-strategy-pairwise-harder-pool.jsonl (generated by evals/datasets/calibrated/build-harder-conceptual-systems-pools.js, m3-harder-data); external: glm-distractor
leakage:
Theory-of-constraints-bottleneck, margin-of-safety-provision, and harder-systems are locally authored datasets — zero external leakage. All target items are locally authored with zero training data leakage risk. GLM distractor items may have training data overlap. Overall leakage risk is very low.

Source breakdown: 0 external dataset(s) with potential training-data leakage; 3 authored dataset(s) with zero external leakage;

label.definition: Paired reasoning quality for systems/product/strategy scenarios. Objective subset: binary bottleneck identification (is the proposed optimization targeting the binding constraint?) and margin-of-safety adequacy (is the provision adequate given uncertainty and failure cost?). Harder items (m3-harder-data) include subtle bottlenecks where the correct stage is non-obvious (close calls, p50 vs p99 latency, Amdahl's law scenarios) and nuanced margin-of-safety judgments. Judge-based items for archetypes, feedback-loops, leverage-points, ooda, and via-negativa are scored by a judge panel.

values.win: Correct binary decision on objectively-gradeable items; skill response preferred by majority of judge panel on behavioral items
values.loss: Incorrect binary decision; placebo response preferred

label.judge_criteria: Compare responses on: diagnostic accuracy, leverage point identification, actionable specificity, and avoidance of symptomatic fixes.

calibration.band_rationale: Per library/harness.md aggregate-vs-per-item rule for binary families: powerability is judged by the AGGREGATE baseline + headroom, NOT per-item-in-band count. Aggregate baseline 57.4% (18 labeled target items at K_TRIALS=5) is squarely in [0.40,0.70], making this family a valid powered candidate. The bimodal per-item distribution (13 ceiling + 5 floor, 0 per-item in-band) is expected for binary classification tasks where a confident solver has strong but sometimes misaligned priors. A skill can flip the systematically-wrong items, producing measurable aggregate lift.
calibration.powered_candidate_note: Valid powered candidate per aggregate band rule. Aggregate baseline 57.4% provides ~43pp headroom for the target ≥5pp lift. Bimodal per-item distribution (13 ceiling + 5 floor) is not disqualifying — the solver answers each binary bottleneck item with ~0% or ~100% confidence, and a skill can flip the systematically-wrong items to produce measurable aggregate lift. This was incorrectly flagged CEILING-NEEDS-HARDER-DATA in the previous manifest revision; corrected to in_band=true per M3 scrutiny fix.
calibration.harder_items_note: The m3-harder-data pool added 18 target items with subtle bottleneck identification (multiple close-call stages, p50 vs p99 latency reasoning, throughput-based capacity analysis, Amdahl's law parallel-stage scenarios) and nuanced margin-of-safety judgments (close-call adequacy with competing signals). The model correctly handled all bottleneck items where the dominant stage was clearly identifiable by numeric comparison (~13 items), but systematically failed on items requiring more nuanced reasoning about parallel stages, tail latency, or hidden bottlenecks. The bimodal pattern suggests the model uses a simple numeric-comparison heuristic rather than systems thinking — it can identify the literal biggest number but struggles with more sophisticated bottleneck theory.
calibration.aggregate_note: Aggregate baseline 57.4% is in [0.40,0.70] — genuine difficulty at the pool level. This family IS a valid powered candidate per library/harness.md aggregate rule for binary families. The per-item bimodal distribution is characteristic of binary classification with high-confidence models and does not disqualify the family from powered runs.

previous_calibration.note: Original calibration on easier items — 100% ceiling. Superseded by harder-pool calibration above.

item_class_definitions.target: Items that directly measure the family value surface — in-domain, skill-relevant items where the skill is expected to help.
item_class_definitions.near_miss: Items that are similar to the family domain but not an exact match. Useful for calibration but may not benefit from the skill.
item_class_definitions.distractor: Items that are clearly off-target (target=false). Used to measure false-positive rate and specificity.
item_class_definitions.ambiguous: Items whose relevance to the family is unclear. Excluded from decisive splits.

pool_limitations.judge_based: This family is largely judge-based/behavioral. Only the objectively-gradeable subset (bottleneck identification + margin-of-safety, 42 target items) can be objectively calibrated. The 29 near-miss items also have labels. The 46 distractor items require judge evaluation for scoring but are included in the decisive split for false-positive measurement (no ground-truth labels). The objective placebo calibration runner cannot profile judge-evaluated items.
pool_limitations.bimodal_calibration: The harder calibration run (calib-systems-harder) showed aggregate baseline 57.4% — valid in-band per library/harness.md aggregate rule for binary families. Per-item distribution is bimodal (13 ceiling, 5 floor) which is expected for binary classification with high-confidence models. The aggregate baseline is what matters for powering.
pool_limitations.powered_candidate_status: VALID POWERED CANDIDATE. Aggregate baseline 57.4% is in [0.40,0.70]. ~43pp headroom available for target ≥5pp lift. The bimodal per-item pattern (ceiling/floor) means the skill can potentially flip systematically-wrong items.
